title: NOC 31202 – Physiotherapists | GTR Immigration
description: Physiotherapists are frontline rehabilitation professionals who play a critical role in restoring movement, function, and quality of life after injury, illness, or surgery. In Canada, physiotherapy services are increasingly essential due to an aging population, higher sports participation, workplace injuries, and post-surgical rehabilitation needs. Classified under NOC 31202 (TEER 1), Physiotherapists qualify for multiple Canada Permanent Residence (PR) pathways, including Express Entry and provincial healthcare streams.
duties: Assess patients’ physical abilities using functional and clinical evaluations
Establish treatment goals based on diagnosis and patient needs

Design and implement physiotherapy treatment plans, including:Therapeutic exercisesJoint mobilization and manipulationMassage therapyElectrotherapy and hydrotherapyPatient education and injury prevention
Evaluate and adjust treatment plans based on patient progress
Provide home exercise and self-management strategies
Communicate with physicians and multidisciplinary healthcare teams
Maintain clinical documentation and treatment records
Develop and deliver health promotion programs
May conduct research or provide consulting and education services
employment_requirements: To work as a Physiotherapist in Canada:
Master’s degree in Physiotherapy is required
Successful completion of the Physiotherapist Competency Examination (PCE)
Registration or licensure with a provincial regulatory body (mandatory in all provinces and territories)
Without provincial licensure, physiotherapy practice is not permitted.
job_outlook: This reflects a stable and balanced labour market, supported by:
🏥 Plan Your Canada PR as a Physiotherapist

GTR Immigration can assist you with NOC 31202 eligibility assessment, credential evaluation, licensing guidance, and Canada PR strategy planning—from Express Entry to province-specific healthcare pathways.
labour_market_demand: Estimated annual income: $48,100 – $95,600 CAD, depending on province, experience, specialization, and private-practice earnings.
Demand is particularly strong in provinces facing healthcare worker shortages and in community-based outpatient care.
pathways: Opportunities for private practice and self-employment
Long-term job stability in Canada’s public healthcare system
